lead:
**Thoshan Flash** is a fine-tuned large language model published on [Hugging Face](https://huggingface.co/lingadevaruhp/thoshan_Flash) by **Lingadevaru H P** (also known as Thoshan, username: lingadevaruhp). It is based on Google's **Gemma-2-9B** and trained using QLoRA (Quantized Low-Rank Adaptation) — a parameter-efficient fine-tuning technique that reduces GPU memory from ~36 GB to ~12 GB.

I want to be upfront: this is **not** a model I built from scratch — it is a fine-tune of an existing pre-trained model. However, it demonstrates real competence in the Hugging Face ecosystem, transformer internals, PEFT methodology, and model deployment.

The model received **28+ downloads in its first month** on the Hugging Face Hub — meaning people beyond me were actually pulling and using it. A live [Gradio inference interface](https://go.lingadevaru.in/LLM) is publicly accessible at [huggingface.co/lingadevaruhp/thoshan_Flash](https://huggingface.co/lingadevaruhp/thoshan_Flash) — no local installation required.

content.title: Live on Hugging Face — 28+ downloads in month one
content.content: Thoshan Flash is publicly available at huggingface.co/lingadevaruhp/thoshan_Flash. The model received 28+ downloads within its first month of publication — indicating real external usage. A Gradio inference interface is deployed alongside the model card for immediate, no-setup usage.

Searching for **Lingadevaru H P**, **lingadevaru-hp**, **lingadevaruhp**, or **Thoshan Flash** on Hugging Face will surface this project. The model card documents the fine-tuning setup, hyperparameters, and intended use case in full.

For the Hugging Face profile covering all published models and datasets, visit [huggingface.co/lingadevaruhp](https://huggingface.co/lingadevaruhp).

sections.title: What QLoRA Does — and Why I Used It

content.content:
Full fine-tuning of a 9B-parameter model requires approximately 36 GB of GPU memory — I don't have that. QLoRA solves this with a three-part approach that I found genuinely elegant:

**Step 1:** Load the base model in 4-bit quantized form, which dramatically reduces memory footprint while preserving most of the model's learned representations.

**Step 2:** Inject small trainable **LoRA adapter layers** at key weight matrices within the transformer. These adapters are the only parts that get updated during training — I'm not touching the 9B base parameters at all.

**Step 3:** At export, merge the trained adapter weights back into the base model, producing a single fine-tuned model file ready for inference.

content.title: Why this matters for resource-constrained training
content.content: LoRA adapters typically represent only 1–3% of the total model parameters. Training 1% of a 9B-parameter model is vastly more resource-efficient than training all 9B parameters — I could run this on hardware that would otherwise be completely inadequate for a model this size.

rows.What I used: Gemma-2-9B (Google) — pre-trained on large text corpus
4-bit → reduced VRAM from ~36 GB to ~12 GB

content.content:
After training, I uploaded the model to the Hugging Face Hub at [huggingface.co/lingadevaruhp/thoshan_Flash](https://huggingface.co/lingadevaruhp/thoshan_Flash) and deployed a Gradio inference interface alongside it, so anyone can run inference without local setup.

Within the first month of publication, I saw **28+ downloads** — people were actually pulling and using the model, not just browsing the model card. This compares favourably with most first-time model publications on the Hub.

content.title: Try it live
content.content: A Gradio interface is deployed at go.lingadevaru.in/LLM and on the Hugging Face model page at huggingface.co/lingadevaruhp/thoshan_Flash. No local installation required.

I deliberately frame Thoshan Flash with accuracy everywhere I present it: this is a fine-tune of an existing model, not something I designed from scratch. I find this distinction important — there's a lot of resume inflation in the AI/ML space where people imply they 'built an LLM' when they really ran a training script on someone else's architecture.

The fine-tuning work connects to my [[about|skill profile]] in AI/ML: parameter-efficient fine-tuning, the Hugging Face ecosystem, and deploying ML models to public inference endpoints. It does not make me a model architect or ML researcher.

For my other ML work using a completely different stack, see [[fraud-detection|Fraud Detection System]] — a traditional ML ensemble I built from scratch in C# for banking fraud classification. For my blockchain work, see [[foss-coin|FOSS Coin]] — a Solana SPL token deployed on Orca DEX.
